titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the John Godfrey Papers
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Kolbe Resnick

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday January 9, 2025 at 01:00

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.physdesc: 5 2.3 inch floppy disks (0.18 gigabytes) and 1 USB flash drive (2.27 gigabytes)
did.abstract:
The John Godfrey papers consists of correspondence, manuscripts, drafts, notebooks, ephemera, photographs, and audiovisual materials. The collection consists of both physical, analog materials and born digital materials.
The collection documents Godfrey's personal life and career as a poet, revealing his writing process and evolution through drafts, letters, journals, notes, and emails. The collection also includes materials showing his relationships with some of the most recognizable figures of the second generation of the New York School poets, poets involved with the Poetry Project at St. Mark's Church in-the-Bowery, as well as figures from other artistic circles in New York from the 1960s to 2022. The material, along with the structure of the collection's organization, reveals his writing process, his inspirations, drafts of poems, editing, and selection process (for publication). Many of the materials are accompanied by Godfrey's handwritten contextualizing notes detailing their relation to Godfrey's published and unpublished works, his personal life, and connections between works in the collection.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Granary Books on the Alfred Z. Baker Fund, 2022.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged into five series: Series I. Correspondence, 1968-2020 Series II. Drafts of work, 1966-2020 Series III. Ephemera, circa 1986-2016 Series IV. Photographs and audiovisual, circa 1980-2006 Series V. Computer media, 1993-2022
The collection has been processed to retain the original order the collection was in when it arrived at the Beinecke. Materials within folders retain the original order.

Prior to arriving at the Beinecke Library, the John Godfrey papers was compiled and organized by the creator, with assistance from Granary Books. The collection arrived with a prospectus detailing the history of the materials and the strategy behind the construction and arrangement of the collection. Extensive notes accompany almost every folder in the collection, highlighting Godfrey's writing process, contextualizing the materials within his career and process, as well as describing what the materials pertain to, if not immediately clear. For this reason, the decision was made to keep the collection in the order it was in, to maintain the structure and contextualization of materials Godfrey intended.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Poet, educator, and nurse John Godfrey was born in Massena, NY in 1945. Following a childhood spent moving around the country with his family, he attended Princeton University, receiving his BA in 1967. At Princeton he began writing and publishing poems, heavily influenced by the poets of the New American Poetry. This brought him to the East Village in Manhattan, where he continued to write and immerse himself in the literary and artistic circles of the ensuing decades. He became involved with the Poetry Project, St. Mark's Church In-the-Bowery, New York, using their mimeograph machine to publish his first two books, 26 Poems (1971) and The Music of the Curbs (1972) (he went on to teach workshops at the Poetry Project on and off from 1974 until 1983). Since then, he has published more than 16 poetry collections and chapbooks, all by small presses. In addition, his work has been featured in numerous journals and magazines, including The Paris Review , Sal Mimeo , The Chicago Review , New American Writing , Vanitas , Talisman , 1913 , New Hunter's Review , Cruel Garters , and A Gathering of Tribes . Godfrey also has numerous collaborations with artist Alex Katz.
In 1984 he was named a General Electric Foundation Fellow, followed by a Foundation for Contemporary Arts grant in 2009. In 2013 he was awarded a fellowship by the Z Foundation.

Godfrey earned a BS in Nursing from Columbia University in 1994, and began a career as a pediatric nurse, working largely with HIV/AIDS patients at hospitals in the Flatbush and East Flatbush neighborhoods of Brooklyn, NY. He retired from nursing in 2011.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: John Godfrey Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The John Godfrey Papers consists of correspondence, notebooks, manuscripts, photographs, writings from contemporaries as well as flyers, gallery announcements, and other ephemera. Also included is a VHS of a reading at Attitude Art in 1987. Digital material consists of computer files, JPGs, manuscript material, personal and work-related documents, and a significant amount of email correspondence with other poets and contemporaries including John Ashbery, Charles Bernstein, Steve Dalachinsky, Richard Hell, Barbara Henning, Erica Hunt, Susan Howe, Alex Katz, Bernadette Mayer, Duncan McNaughton, Alice Notley, Maureen Owen, Anne Waldman, and Lewis Warsh, among others. The paper material covers the years 1966-2022 with the bulk of the material dating from 1985-1997. Born digital material covers 1993-2022. Transferred to the Beinecke on five floppy disks and one USB flash drive, the computer files consist of manuscript poems, drafts, digital proofs and layouts, photographs, artwork, personal documents, and personal and professional correspondence.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The John Godfrey papers are the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I. contains correspondence with contemporary poets, authors, friends, and family members. Documented in the correspondence is Godfrey's close relationships with other poets and artists as well as their admiration for his work. Also included is correspondence and journal entries from his extended travels in Thailand during the late 1980s and legal correspondence following a car accident in 1986.

The written correspondence is a small part of the overall correspondence. The majority will be found in the email sub-series, which holds over 4,500 emails and covers the years 2013-2022, documenting Godfrey's relationships with other New York City poets, artists, and scholars. Topics range from book reviews, publication correspondence, business emails, personal updates, health-related questions posed to Godfrey from his peers, and event announcements. The emails also provide a snapshot of the New York literary community's life and adaptation during lockdown due to the Covid-19 pandemic, when many in-person events were canceled or moved online.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Public Services for further information.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Prior to being transferred to the Beinecke, John Godfrey's emails were curated and arranged by Godfrey and Granary books to remove any mass marketing or irrelevant emails. Godfrey sent two .mbox files arranged into "Inbox" and "Sent", that consist of a total of 4,533 emails dating from 2013 to 2022. The .mbox files were included on the USB flash drive supplied by Granary with the computer media. Godfrey's emails were processed by Beinecke staff using the email appraisal software, ePADD.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Covering the years 2013-2022, John Godfrey's email inbox includes correspondence with many notable figures in the literary and artistic circles of New York and beyond. Topics range from book reviews, publication correspondence, business emails, personal updates, health-related questions posed to Godfrey from his peers, and event announcements. The emails also provide a snapshot of the New York literary community's life and adaptation during lockdown due to the covid-19 pandemic, when many in-person events were canceled or moved online. Most frequent correspondents include Ron Padgett, Angelica Clark, Bill Berkson, Alice Notley, Peter Schjeldhal, Richard Hell, Simon Pettet, and Greg Masters. Other correspondents include Penny Arcade, John Ashbery, Vyt Bakaitis, Micah Ballard, Joshua Beckman, Alan Bernheimer, Anselm Berrigan, Edmund Berrigan, Sheelagh Bevan, Elizabeth Botten, Garret Caples, Charlotte Carter, Miles Champion, John Coletti, Susan Coolidge, Kyle Dacuyan, Steve Dalachinsky, Jane Dalrymple-Hollo, Alan Davies, Thomas Devaney, Martha Diamond, Lila Dlaboha, Joan Fagin, Olivia Funk, Cliff Fyman, Peter Gizzi, Ted Greenwald, David Henderson, Barbara Henning, Bob Holman, Alex Katz, Vincent Katz, Basil King, John Koethe, Michael Lally, Theodora Lang, Annabel Lee, Duncan McNaughton, Elinor Nauen, Susan Noel, Kevin Opstedal, Phoebe Ozun, Laurie Price, Carter Ratcliff, Kit Robinson, Karin Roffman, Harris Schiff, Kyle Schlesinger, Simon Schucha, Ann Stephenson, Nick Sturm, Stacy Szymaszek, Tony Towle, Morgan Vo, Anne Waldman, Lewis Warsh, Marie Warsh, Trevor Winkfield, John Yau, Don Yorty, Bill Zavatsky, among others. Also included with the emails are over 600 attachments (many in the form of .jpg and .doc) of images and documents, including drafts, manuscripts, proofs/layouts for publications, gallery checklists, and flyers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II. Drafts of work includes autograph and typed manuscripts of poems, written on a variety of writing surfaces, including notebooks, sketchbooks, loose sheets of paper, envelopes, and notecards. The writings consist of individual poems, completed manuscripts, journal entries, essays, drafts, reviews, tributes, blog posts, collaborations, and notes of inspiration. Individual poems selected, considered, and rejected for larger works, some with significant edits and markings are included and in most cases noted by Godfrey.

did.unittitle: Scarecrow Dollar [ A Torch for Orphans ], autograph manuscript
Midnight on Your Left , typescript, corrected
City Keeps , photocopied poems with notes, corrections
Review of Poems for the Entire Family
[Piece on Holly Solomon's 98 Green St. Project Readings]
Notebook [ Rhodia File , Tiny Gold Dress ]
Going to See Allen Ginsburg Read Poems [translation], typescript
Poems [autograph manuscript, written on blank envelopes]

title.part: At the Stroke of Katz, Sharp

did.unittitle: Midnight on Your Left , typescript, uncorrected

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III consists of flyers, blank postcards, and gallery announcements as well as clippings featuring reviews of some of Godfrey's publications.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV contains professional portraits of John Godfrey with some photographs of family and friends. Also included is a VHS tape with a recording of a live reading Godfrey gave at Attitude Art in New York City in 1987.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Public Services for further information.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Born-digital materials were transferred to the Beinecke on five floppy disks and one USB flash drive and accessioned through the Yale University's Digital Accessioning Support Service (DASS) in 2022. Disk images were created from the materials and Beinecke staff processed the materials using Quick View Plus and FTK (Forensic Toolkit) Imager software. Since the selection of files and their arrangement was completed by Godfrey prior to transfer, no work was done to remove or rearrange files on the disks and all metadata has been largely left intact with file manifests generated upon receipt of the material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Computer files consist of manuscript poems, drafts, digital proofs and layouts, photographs, artwork, personal documents, and personal and professional correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains drafts of poems, manuscripts, flyers, artwork for collections, photographs, proposals, as well as personal documents.

did.physdesc: 819 Kilobytes. This reflects the size of the carrier media, but may not accurately reflect the extent of digital files available for research.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains journal entries from early on in Godfrey's nursing career.

did.physdesc: 819 Kilobytes. This reflects the size of the carrier media, but may not accurately reflect the extent of digital files available for research.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains untitled files and folders from Godfrey's desktop.

819 Kilobytes. This reflects the size of the carrier media, but may not accurately reflect the extent of digital files available for research.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains a draft of Push the Mule.
